name: Amaterasu
title: Japanese - Amaterasu
symbols: Rooster (Niwatori) - whose crow summoned her from the cave
epithets: Amaterasu-Omikami (天照大神) - "Great August Kami Who Shines in Heaven"
Ohirume-no-Muchi (大日霪貴) - "Great Sun Female Deity"

relationships.mother: born not from izanami but from izanagi's left eye during his misogi purification rite
children:
ancestor of the imperial line
        gr
son: ninigi-no-mikoto - sent to rule earth
relationships.father: izanagi-no-mikoto - the male creator deity who gave birth to her through purification

epithets: Amaterasu-Omikami (天照大神) - "Great August Kami Who Shines in Heaven"
Ohirume-no-Muchi (大日霪貴) - "Great Sun Female Deity"
symbols: Rooster (Niwatori) - whose crow summoned her from the cave

description: Also known as Amaterasu-Omikami (天照大神). Associated with Sun, light, warmth. Known by the epithets: Amaterasu-Omikami (天照大神) - "Great August Kami Who Shines in Heaven".

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu's Birth
keyMyths.description: Amaterasu emerged from the left eye of Izanagi as he purified himself after visiting Yomi, the land of the dead. Her birth signified the creation of the sun and the heavens, establishing her divine authority.

keyMyths.title: Amaterasu's Cave Retreat
keyMyths.description: After a series of pranks by her brother Susano-o, the storm god, Amaterasu retreated into Ama-no-Iwato (Heavenly Rock Cave), plunging the world into darkness. The other gods devised a plan involving a boisterous celebration and a mirror to lure her out.

keyMyths.title: Susano-o's Rampage and Amaterasu's Grief
keyMyths.description: Susano-o's destructive actions, including destroying Amaterasu's rice fields and flinging a flayed pony into her weaving hall, deeply angered and saddened her. This led to her hiding in the cave, demonstrating the importance of harmony and the consequences of chaos.

keyMyths.title: The Mirror and the Sacred Jewels
keyMyths.description: The mirror (Yata no Kagami) was used to entice Amaterasu from the cave, and along with the sword (Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi) and jewel (Yasakani no Magatama), became part of the Imperial Regalia of Japan. These objects symbolize wisdom, valor, and benevolence, and legitimize the imperial line's divine ancestry from Amaterasu.

sources.title: Myths of Pre-Modern Japan: Ideologies, Rites, and Symbols
sources.author: Donald L. Philippi

sources.title: A Study of the Mythology of Japan
sources.author: Masaharu Anesaki

extendedContent.title: Origins
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu's birth is a pivotal event in Japanese mythology, detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the earliest chronicles of Japan. She is born from the left eye of Izanagi, the creator god, as he purified himself after his descent into Yomi, the underworld, to retrieve his deceased wife, Izanami. This act of purification and the specific origin from Izanagi's eye immediately establishes Amaterasu's association with purity, light, and the heavens. Her siblings, Tsukuyomi (the moon god) and Susanoo (the storm god), were born from Izanagi's right eye and nose respectively, completing the triad of important deities.

The circumstances of Amaterasu's birth are significant as they predetermine her role and authority. Being born from Izanagi's eye, rather than through a natural birth, elevates her status above ordinary deities. This divine origin justifies her position as the ruler of the Takamagahara, the High Celestial Plain, and the supreme deity of the Shinto pantheon. The creation myth thus legitimizes the imperial lineage's claim to divine descent from Amaterasu.

Furthermore, the relationships between Amaterasu and her siblings are fraught with conflict, particularly with Susanoo. These sibling rivalries represent fundamental forces in the cosmos and contribute to the dynamic narrative of Japanese mythology. The tension between light (Amaterasu) and storm (Susanoo) is a recurring theme, highlighting the balance and interplay of opposing forces in the world. These early narratives establish the foundation for Amaterasu's later actions and her enduring importance in Japanese culture.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu is the central deity in Shintoism, and her worship is deeply ingrained in Japanese culture. The Ise Grand Shrine (Ise Jingu) in Mie Prefecture is the most important Shinto shrine in Japan, dedicated to Amaterasu. It is rebuilt every 20 years in a ritual called Shikinen Sengu, symbolizing renewal and the enduring power of the goddess. This tradition, dating back over a millennium, underscores the unwavering devotion to Amaterasu and the importance of maintaining her sacred space.

The imperial family plays a crucial role in Amaterasu's worship. They are believed to be direct descendants of Amaterasu, thus lending divine legitimacy to their rule. The emperor traditionally performs rituals and ceremonies to honor Amaterasu and ensure the prosperity of the nation. The Yata no Kagami (the sacred mirror), one of the Three Sacred Treasures of Japan, is housed at Ise Grand Shrine and is considered the most important physical representation of Amaterasu. It symbolizes wisdom and honesty and is central to imperial succession ceremonies.

Beyond Ise Grand Shrine, numerous other shrines throughout Japan are dedicated to Amaterasu. These shrines vary in size and significance, but they all serve as places of worship and reverence for the sun goddess. Offerings of rice, sake, and other agricultural products are commonly made to Amaterasu, reflecting her association with fertility and abundance. Festivals celebrating the sun, such as the winter solstice, often incorporate elements of Amaterasu's worship, reinforcing her connection to the natural cycles and the well-being of the people.

extendedContent.title: Iconography
extendedContent.content:
Amaterasu is most commonly represented by the sun itself, embodying her role as the source of light and warmth. Direct depictions of Amaterasu are rare in traditional iconography, reflecting the sacred and revered nature of the goddess. However, when she is depicted in human form, she is often portrayed as a radiant and beautiful woman, adorned in elegant robes and surrounded by light. She may be shown holding or associated with the Yata no Kagami, the sacred mirror, which symbolizes her wisdom and connection to the divine realm.

The Yata no Kagami is a crucial element in Amaterasu's iconography. It is not merely a reflection of her appearance but also a representation of truth and self-reflection. The mirror's ability to reveal one's true nature aligns with Amaterasu's association with honesty and justice. In some depictions, Amaterasu is shown emerging from the Ama-no-Iwato, the rock cave where she hid herself, plunging the world into darkness. This imagery emphasizes her power to control the sun and her importance in maintaining the balance of the cosmos.

Animals associated with the sun, such as the rooster, are also indirectly linked to Amaterasu. The rooster's crowing is believed to herald the dawn, signifying the return of light and the dispelling of darkness. The use of gold and other shimmering materials in artwork and religious objects can also be seen as symbolic representations of Amaterasu's radiant energy and her association with the sun. While direct depictions may be scarce, the symbolic representations of Amaterasu are pervasive throughout Japanese art and culture.

worshipPractices.name: Offering of Sacred Items
worshipPractices.description: Amaterasu is honored with offerings of sacred items such as mirrors (yata no kagami), jewels (yasakani no magatama), and swords (kusanagi-no-tsurugi), which are symbolic of her power and role in the imperial regalia. These items are believed to embody her divine presence and are offered to ensure her continued blessings and light upon the world. Purity and cleanliness are paramount in these rituals.

worshipPractices.name: Purification Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Because Amaterasu represents light and purity, purification rituals are crucial in her worship. These rituals aim to cleanse individuals and spaces of impurity (kegare) to ensure they are worthy to receive her blessings and presence. Water, salt, and fire are often used in these cleansing practices.

festivals.name: Shunki Koreisai (Spring Imperial Ancestral Ritual)
festivals.description: This festival honors the Imperial ancestors, including Amaterasu, and prays for a bountiful harvest. The Emperor leads the ceremonies, which include offerings and prayers for the prosperity of the nation and its people. It's a time for reflection on the lineage of the Imperial family and its connection to the divine.

festivals.name: Niiname-sai (Harvest Festival)
festivals.description: The Emperor offers newly harvested rice to Amaterasu and other deities, then partakes in the first tasting of the new crop. This ritual expresses gratitude for the harvest and seeks blessings for continued abundance. It's a sacred event that connects the Emperor to the agricultural cycle and the divine.

cultural.modernLegacy: Amaterasu remains a significant figure in Japanese culture, often depicted in art, literature, and manga. Her story and symbolism are still referenced in modern media, reinforcing her position as a powerful and benevolent deity. The Ise Grand Shrine, dedicated to Amaterasu, is a major pilgrimage site and a symbol of Japanese national identity.

associations.name: Sun
associations.significance: Amaterasu is the goddess of the sun, embodying its life-giving energy.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Gold represents the radiant and precious nature of the sun and Amaterasu's divine power.

associations.name: Enlightenment
associations.significance: Amaterasu's light dispels darkness, symbolizing knowledge and understanding.

associations.name: Rice
associations.significance: Rice, a staple food in Japan, symbolizes Amaterasu's role in providing sustenance and prosperity.

associations.name: Fire
associations.significance: Fire represents the sun's power and warmth, reflecting Amaterasu's energy and influence.

associations.name: Rooster
associations.significance: The rooster's crow heralds the dawn, symbolizing Amaterasu's return and the triumph of light over darkness.

associations.name: Order
associations.significance: Amaterasu's role in the heavenly realm signifies the establishment and maintenance of cosmic order.

companions.name: Ame-no-Uzume
companions.description: Ame-no-Uzume is the goddess of dawn, mirth, and revelry who famously danced to lure Amaterasu out of the cave. She is a key figure in restoring light to the world.

companions.name: Ame-no-Koyane
companions.description: Ame-no-Koyane is a god of literature and wisdom who recited powerful prayers and incantations outside the cave to encourage Amaterasu to emerge, showcasing his devotion to her.

companions.name: Futodama
companions.description: Futodama is a god associated with divination and rituals who held the sacred jewels and mirror to entice Amaterasu from the cave, emphasizing his role in facilitating her return.

companions.name: Yata no Kagami
companions.description: The Yata no Kagami is a sacred mirror used to lure Amaterasu out of the cave. It reflects her own radiant light, symbolizing truth and self-recognition.

companions.name: Ame-no-Iwato
companions.description: While technically an object, the Ame-no-Iwato is the heavenly rock cave where Amaterasu hid. It served as her refuge and a place of power.

shortDescription: Amaterasu-Omikami is the sun goddess in Japanese mythology. She is the most important Shinto deity, ruler of the heavens, and ancestor of the Imperial line.
subtitle: The radiant sun goddess, illuminating the heavens and the world.
